nativeRange.habitat: Reef-associated, found in lagoons and outer reef slopes at depths of roughly 1-18 m, typically sheltering inside a host anemone.
nativeRange.biotope: Tropical Indo-Pacific reef and lagoon; coral and rocky reef areas hosting bubble-tip and leathery sea anemones.

decorPreferences: live rock with caves and overhangs
open mid-water swimming space to establish a territory
host anemone optional (Entacmaea quadricolor bubble-tip preferred, Heteractis crispa also accepted)

diet.notes: Generally accepts most prepared marine fare. A varied diet of frozen mysis, brine shrimp, marine pellets, flake foods and herbivore preparations is commonly recommended. In the wild has been documented feeding on planktonic copepods and algae.

goodWith: peaceful and semi-aggressive reef fish of similar or smaller size
reef-safe invertebrates including shrimps and snails
avoidWith: other clownfish species in the same tank
small timid species that can be bullied at feeding time
large predatory fish that may eat small clownfish

imageCandidates.notes: Ditch Townsend, 2048x1532. Striking, tack-sharp in-situ shot of a vibrant single adult in a green host anemone; clearly shows the diagnostic cinnamon-red body, black flank saddle, single bluish-white head bar and yellow/orange fins.
Julien Renoult, ~1024px large. Vibrant, sharp whole-body view from the opposite angle; red body with black saddle, single blue-white bar and bright yellow caudal/pelvic fins on a reef backdrop.

Nick Hobgood, 2400x1800. Group of several individuals nestled in a bubble-tip anemone (Entacmaea quadricolor); shows the species in its natural host context with clear red bodies, black flanks and single white bars.

summary: The Cinnamon Clownfish (Amphiprion melanopus) is a member of the Tomato Complex — a group of bold, territorial Indo-Pacific clownfish — distinguished by a rich cinnamon-red body, darkened flanks, and a single white or bluish head bar. In the wild it ranges from Indonesia and the Philippines east to Micronesia and the Coral Sea, sheltering inside host anemones.
careNotes: The Cinnamon Clownfish (Amphiprion melanopus, described by Bleeker in 1852) belongs to the Tomato Complex, a group of clownfish known for a bold, territorial disposition. It tends to be peaceful toward unrelated tankmates but can be aggressively territorial toward other clownfish or similarly shaped damselfish. It is best kept singly or as a single bonded pair, and should not be mixed with other clownfish species in the same aquarium. Like all clownfish, it is a protandrous sequential hermaphrodite — meaning it begins life as a male and can change sex to female. In a bonded pair the larger fish is the female and the smaller is the functional male. Host anemones are not required in captivity, but the species readily associates with Entacmaea quadricolor (the bubble-tip anemone) and Heteractis crispa, and has been reported to associate with Heteractis magnifica on occasion, when offered. Juveniles display a thin white head bar that can broaden and shift toward bluish in adults, while the flanks and pelvic fins deepen to black and the head and abdomen retain a rich cinnamon-red colouration. The species is generally considered very hardy and a good entry-level marine fish. Captive-bred specimens, available from a number of aquaculture producers, tend to be more acclimated to prepared foods and aquarium conditions than wild-caught individuals. New aquarists are commonly advised to add the Cinnamon Clownfish last when stocking a new tank, giving more timid tankmates time to establish themselves before this fish claims a territory.
breedingNotes: The Cinnamon Clownfish has been reared at commercial scale by aquaculture producers and is generally considered moderately challenging to breed, comparable in difficulty to other Tomato Complex clownfish. It is an egg-layer that deposits demersal eggs (laid on a hard surface rather than scattered into open water) on a cleaned flat surface, typically near the host anemone, with the male guarding and aerating the clutch until hatching. Clutches average around 250 eggs per spawn. Eggs hatch in roughly 8 to 11 days depending on temperature, and the larvae require rotifer cultures followed by enriched newly-hatched brine shrimp in a dedicated rearing tank.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Primary species page (Proaquatix Captive-Bred Cinnamon Clownfish). The listed root URL https://www.liveaquaria.com/collections/clownfish did not surface this species on its first catalog page; the canonical product page was located via site search.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked scientific name (Bleeker, 1852), family (Pomacentridae), max size (12 cm TL), preferred temperature range (25.1-29.3°C, mean 28.7°C), Indo-Pacific distribution (Indonesia, Philippines, PNG, Coral Sea, Solomon Islands, Vanuatu, New Caledonia, Palau, Micronesia), depth range (1-18 m), wild diet (planktonic copepods and algae), and host-anemone associations (Entacmaea quadricolor, Heteractis crispa, occasionally H. magnifica). FishBase lists max age of 5 years; captive lifespan is typically longer and the species variant uses the longer captive figure.

additional.name: ORA (Oceans, Reefs & Aquariums)
additional.notes: Third-source confirmation of Tomato Complex placement, color phases (black saddle on red body, yellow/orange fins, blue head stripe in adults vs. white juvenile stripe), New Caledonia origin (also marketed as Coral Sea Clownfish), and breeding biology (clutches averaging ~250 eggs with larval development comparable to Tomato Clownfish).

additional.name: AquariumDomain species profile
additional.notes: Fourth-source confirmation that A. melanopus is one of the larger Western Pacific clownfish (5-6 in / 13-15 cm reported), semi-aggressive temperament (notably more territorial than A. ocellaris), recommendation to add last so timid tankmates establish first, and that host anemones (E. quadricolor, H. crispa) are preferred but not required for captive-bred specimens. Used in the adult-size cross-source spread.

additional.name: Reef2Reef wiki
additional.notes: Designated secondary in source-urls.json for fish-saltwater slice. Per-species wiki page not separately verified beyond the general clownfish discussion thread; AquariumDomain and ORA were substituted as additional cross-verification sources.

fish.breedingNotes: Captive-bred at commercial scale by established aquaculture producers. Demersal egg-layer; clutches of around 250 eggs are common. Larvae require rotifer cultures followed by enriched brine shrimp in a dedicated rearing tank.
